title: Building Modern Web Apps with Vanilla JavaScript
excerpt: Discover how to build powerful, performant web applications without relying on heavy frameworks. Vanilla JavaScript has matured significantly and offers everything you need.
author: Anirudh Tavva

title: CSS Custom Properties: The Complete Guide to Dynamic Theming
excerpt: CSS custom properties (variables) are one of the most powerful features in modern CSS. Learn how to use them to build flexible, maintainable stylesheets and dynamic themes.
author: Anirudh Tavva

title: The Art of Web Performance: Core Web Vitals Explained
excerpt: Core Web Vitals are Google's set of specific metrics for measuring user experience. Understanding and optimizing them can dramatically improve both user satisfaction and SEO rankings.
content: ## Why Performance Matters Web performance is not just a technical concern — it directly impacts business outcomes. Studies consistently show that: - A 1-second delay in page load time can result in a 7% reduction in conversions - 53% of mobile users abandon sites that take longer than 3 seconds to load - Google uses Core Web Vitals as a ranking signal ## The Three Core Web Vitals ### Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) LCP measures *loading performance* — specifically, how long it takes for the largest content element visible in the viewport to render. **Good**: Less than 2.5 seconds **Needs Improvement**: 2.5 to 4.0 seconds **Poor**: More than 4.0 seconds Common LCP elements include: - Hero images - Large text blocks - Video poster images ```javascript // Measure LCP with PerformanceObserver new PerformanceObserver((entryList) => { const entries = entryList.getEntries(); const lastEntry = entries[entries.length - 1]; console.log('LCP:', lastEntry.startTime); }).observe({ type: 'largest-contentful-paint', buffered: true }); ``` ### Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS) CLS measures *visual stability* — how much page elements move unexpectedly during loading. **Good**: Less than 0.1 **Needs Improvement**: 0.1 to 0.25 **Poor**: More than 0.25 Common causes of poor CLS: - Images without explicit dimensions - Ads that inject content dynamically - Web fonts that cause text to reflow - Dynamically injected content ```css /* Always specify image dimensions to prevent layout shift */ img { width: 100%; height: auto; aspect-ratio: 16 / 9; /* Or use explicit height */ } ``` ### Interaction to Next Paint (INP) INP replaced FID (First Input Delay) in 2024. It measures the latency of *all* interactions throughout the page lifecycle, not just the first one. **Good**: Less than 200 milliseconds **Needs Improvement**: 200 to 500 milliseconds **Poor**: More than 500 milliseconds ## Optimization Strategies ### Image Optimization Images are often the largest assets on a page. Optimize them aggressively: ```html ``` ### Critical CSS Inline critical CSS to avoid render-blocking stylesheets: ```html ``` ### JavaScript Optimization ```javascript // Use code splitting - load heavy features on demand async function loadChart() { const { Chart } = await import('./chart.js'); return new Chart(document.getElementById('canvas')); } // Debounce expensive operations function debounce(fn, delay) { let timer; return (...args) => { clearTimeout(timer); timer = setTimeout(() => fn(...args), delay); }; } window.addEventListener('resize', debounce(handleResize, 150)); ``` ## Measuring Performance Use these tools to measure and monitor your Core Web Vitals: - **Lighthouse**: Built into Chrome DevTools - **PageSpeed Insights**: Google's online tool with field data - **WebPageTest**: Detailed waterfall analysis - **Chrome UX Report**: Real user data from Chrome users > "Performance is a feature. Every millisecond counts when it comes to user experience." --- Start with measuring your current metrics, identify the biggest opportunities, and work through them systematically. Small improvements compound over time.
author: Anirudh Tavva

title: Designing for Accessibility: Beyond the Basics
excerpt: Accessibility is not an afterthought — it's a core design principle. Learn how to make your websites truly inclusive with practical techniques that benefit all users.
author: Anirudh Tavva

title: Git Workflows for Solo Developers and Small Teams
excerpt: A well-defined Git workflow can be the difference between a smooth development process and a chaotic mess of conflicts and lost work. Here's what actually works.
content:
## The Problem with Ad-Hoc Git Usage

Most developers learn Git commands before they learn Git *workflows*. The result? Repositories with cryptic commit messages, massive commits that do twelve things at once, and a history that's impossible to debug.

A good workflow doesn't slow you down — it makes you faster by reducing the time spent untangling problems.

## Commit Message Conventions

The Conventional Commits specification is a lightweight standard that makes your commit history useful:

```bash
# Format: <type>(<scope>): <description>

feat(auth): add OAuth2 login with GitHub
fix(api): handle null response from user endpoint
docs(readme): update installation instructions
style(nav): fix mobile menu alignment
refactor(store): simplify state update logic
test(auth): add unit tests for token refresh
chore(deps): bump lodash from 4.17.20 to 4.17.21
```

The types are: `feat`, `fix`, `docs`, `style`, `refactor`, `test`, `chore`, `perf`, `ci`

## The Feature Branch Workflow

For solo developers and small teams, a simple feature branch workflow is usually sufficient:

```bash
# Start from an up-to-date main branch
git checkout main
git pull origin main

# Create a feature branch
git checkout -b feat/user-authentication

# Work on the feature with focused commits
git add src/auth/
git commit -m "feat(auth): add login form component"

git add src/api/
git commit -m "feat(auth): add authentication API calls"

# Push and open a PR (even for solo work — for review purposes)
git push -u origin feat/user-authentication

# After review, merge and clean up
git checkout main
git merge --squash feat/user-authentication
git commit -m "feat(auth): implement user authentication (#42)"
git branch -d feat/user-authentication
```

## Interactive Rebase: Your Secret Weapon

Before merging, clean up your commit history with interactive rebase:

```bash
# Rebase the last 4 commits interactively
git rebase -i HEAD~4

# In the editor, you can:
# pick   - keep the commit as is
# reword - keep commit, but edit the message
# edit   - stop to amend the commit
# squash - combine with previous commit
# fixup  - like squash, but discard this commit's message
# drop   - remove the commit
```

## Git Aliases for Power Users

Add these to your `~/.gitconfig`:

```bash
[alias]
  # Prettier log
  lg = log --color --graph --pretty=format:'%Cred%h%Creset -%C(yellow)%d%Creset %s %Cgreen(%cr) %C(bold blue)<%an>%Creset' --abbrev-commit
  
  # Undo last commit, keep changes staged
  undo = reset --soft HEAD~1
  
  # Show files changed in last commit
  last = diff HEAD~1 HEAD --name-only
  
  # Stash with a message
  ss = stash save
  
  # List all branches with last commit date
  branches = for-each-ref --sort=-committerdate refs/heads/ --format='%(refname:short) %(committerdate:relative)'
```

## The .gitignore Strategy

A clean `.gitignore` prevents accidental commits of sensitive or generated files:

```bash
# Environment and secrets
.env
.env.local
.env.*.local
*.key
*.pem

# Dependencies
node_modules/
vendor/

# Build output
dist/
build/
.next/

# Editor files
.vscode/settings.json
.idea/
*.swp
*.swo

# OS files
.DS_Store
Thumbs.db
```

## Handling Merge Conflicts

Merge conflicts are inevitable. Here's a systematic approach:

```bash
# When you hit a conflict, first understand what's happening
git status  # See which files have conflicts
git diff    # See the actual conflicts

# Open conflicted files and resolve manually
# Then mark as resolved
git add resolved-file.js

# Continue the merge
git merge --continue

# If things go wrong, abort and start over
git merge --abort
```

## Tagging Releases

Use annotated tags for releases:

```bash
# Create annotated tag
git tag -a v1.2.0 -m "Release version 1.2.0

- Add user authentication
- Fix mobile navigation bug
- Improve page load performance"

# Push tags to remote
git push origin --tags
```

> "Clean history is a gift to your future self and your teammates."

---

A good Git workflow is one that your team actually follows. Start simple, be consistent, and evolve the workflow as your team's needs change.
author: Anirudh Tavva
